common_name: Thelma Sanders Sweet Potato Squash (Missouri Heirloom Acorn-Type with Sweet Nutty Flesh)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Thelma Sanders Sweet Potato Squash produces medium-sized fruits (2–3 lbs) with a form similar to acorn squash but with cream to tan skin rather than acorn squash's dark green, and the characteristic lightly ridged, oval to oblong shape. In zone 9, plant March–April for summer production or July–August for a fall crop that matures in October–November when cooler weather improves flavor and texture. The cream skin color deepens to tan as the squash fully cures, providing a visual harvest maturity cue. Thelma Sanders is an open-pollinated heirloom available through Seed Savers Exchange — it originated with Thelma Sanders of Missouri who maintained the seed before the Seed Savers Exchange helped distribute it more broadly, a typical pattern for regional heirloom vegetable varieties preserved by individual gardeners.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in hills; thin to 2 plants per hill. Harvest when skin is tan-cream and stem has dried. Thelma Sanders Sweet Potato Squash is a Missouri heirloom winter squash with an unusually devoted following among winter squash enthusiasts — one of the open-pollinated acorn-type squashes most frequently cited for superior eating quality among the many heirloom varieties preserved through the Seed Savers Exchange network. The variety produces oval to oblong fruits, 6–8 inches long, with smooth to lightly ridged cream to tan skin that turns uniformly tan at full maturity. The name 'sweet potato squash' references the flesh character: the texture is drier and denser than standard acorn squash, with a sweet, slightly dry, nutty quality reminiscent in some ways of baked sweet potato — particularly the way the flesh separates into fibrous, sweet sections when baked. This dry, dense texture is highly valued by cooks who find standard acorn squash flesh too watery: Thelma Sanders bakes to a fluffy, dry interior that absorbs butter and seasonings distinctly better than wet-fleshed varieties, and the sweetness at full maturity is exceptional. The flavor is genuinely complex — sweet, nutty, slightly starchy — in a way that distinguishes it from hybrid acorn types selected for uniformity and yield rather than flavor depth. Thelma Sanders' origin traces to an individual Missouri gardener of the same name who maintained the seed through her personal gardening before sharing it through the Seed Savers Exchange community.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Thelma Sanders sweet potato squash Missouri heirloom documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Thelma Sanders squash variety notes and flavor description
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Heritage winter squash variety comparison

common_name: Jarrahdale Squash (Australian Heritage Blue-Grey Pumpkin with Dense Sweet Orange Flesh)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Jarrahdale is a Cucurbita maxima type that requires at least 95 days and performs well in zone 9's long growing season. Plant March–April for October harvest. The distinctive flat, deeply-ribbed, blue-grey skin of Jarrahdale does not change color significantly at harvest maturity — look instead for dried stem, firm rind, and overall size (typically 7–10 lbs when ripe). The blue-grey skin of Jarrahdale is unique and is associated with a specific group of Australian heritage pumpkins that produce the grey-blue skin color from a specific combination of pigments and wax bloom; this color group includes Queensland Blue, Crown Prince, and other varieties sharing similar heritage and the distinctive blue-grey appearance. The flesh is deep orange, dense, dry, and very sweet — particularly well suited for pumpkin soups, curries, pies, and roasting where a dry, high-starch pumpkin flesh is preferred.

directions: Direct sow in hills after last frost; harvest when stem dries and skin is firm grey-blue. Jarrahdale Squash (also called Jarrahdale Pumpkin in its native Australia, where 'pumpkin' is the standard term for what North Americans call both pumpkin and winter squash) is an Australian heritage variety named after the town of Jarrahdale in the Darling Ranges southeast of Perth, Western Australia, where the variety was grown and maintained by local families before being recognized and distributed more widely. The Jarrahdale pumpkin belongs to a distinctive Australian heritage pumpkin group characterized by grey-blue skin — a color produced by a combination of pigmentation and a powdery wax bloom on the skin surface — that distinguishes these varieties from the orange and tan pumpkins dominating North American markets. This blue-grey color group, which includes Queensland Blue, Crown Prince (United Kingdom), and related European heritage varieties, represents a distinct aesthetic and culinary tradition in which the blue skin and dense, dry orange flesh are valued for specific culinary properties. The flesh of Jarrahdale is particularly dry and dense for a large pumpkin — significantly less watery than most carving pumpkins and many North American pie pumpkins — which makes it exceptional for preparations where moisture content affects texture: pumpkin soup (concentrated flavor without excessive water thinning), pumpkin curry, and baked preparations where excess moisture causes sogginess. The flavor is genuinely sweet and complex, with a depth that makes Jarrahdale one of the most culinarily respected heritage pumpkin varieties in Australian cooking and in North American heirloom vegetable circles.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Jarrahdale pumpkin Australian heritage variety notes
Seed Savers Exchange — Blue-grey pumpkin heritage variety documentation
Royal Horticultural Society — Cucurbita maxima heritage pumpkin variety comparison

common_name: Sunshine Squash (AAS-Winning Bright Orange Kabocha with Rich Sweet Flesh)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Sunshine is a kabocha-type Cucurbita maxima producing small to medium fruits (3–5 lbs) with a distinctive bright orange skin — virtually identical in form to red kuri / Uchiki Kuri except with orange skin rather than red-orange. In zone 9, plant March–April for a fall harvest (October–November) as the squash approaches maturity in cooler fall weather that improves sweetness. The skin of fully ripe Sunshine changes from mottled orange-tan at immature stages to a uniform, vivid orange at harvest. The small size (3–5 lbs) makes Sunshine practical for single-household use without the waste of cutting into a very large squash. Sunshine received All-America Selections (AAS) recognition for consistent performance across North American trial locations, including warm climates where some kabocha types underperform.

directions: Direct sow in hills 3–4 feet apart; thin to 2 plants per hill. Harvest when fully vivid orange and stem begins to dry. Sunshine Squash is an F1 hybrid kabocha-type winter squash that received All-America Selections recognition for its exceptional productivity, vibrant visual appearance, and excellent eating quality in the kabocha class — a category of Cucurbita maxima squash that includes some of the highest-rated winter squash varieties for flavor intensity, flesh texture, and sweetness. Kabocha squash (from the Japanese name for pumpkin, itself derived from 'Cambodia' in Portuguese, reflecting the trade route by which the squash reached Japan) have been developed in Japan into a distinctive style — typically flattened globe form, dense, dry, sweet orange to yellow-orange flesh, and rich, complex chestnut-like flavor — that differs substantially from the North American traditions of summer squash (Cucurbita pepo) and large pumpkin (C. maxima). Sunshine's orange skin sets it visually apart from the dark green and grey-green Japanese kabocha types that inspired it — the vivid orange exterior makes it immediately recognizable at harvest and provides a decorative harvest-season display function alongside its culinary utility. The flesh is dense, dry (low water content), very sweet at full maturity, and with the characteristic kabocha texture that some cooks describe as between sweet potato and chestnut — a distinctive dry, starchy sweetness that makes kabocha the preferred squash for soups, tempura, and Japanese-inspired preparations where excess moisture would dilute the flavors.
sources: All-America Selections — Sunshine squash AAS winner recognition and trial documentation
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Sunshine kabocha squash F1 variety notes
Harris Seeds — Sunshine squash commercial performance comparison

common_name: North Georgia Candy Roaster (Southern Appalachian Cherokee Heritage Winter Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; North Georgia Candy Roaster is a very large squash (15–30 lbs) requiring 100–120 days and substantial space (vines 12+ feet). In zone 9, plant March–April for October–November harvest. The variety is cold-sensitive as a young plant but very heat-tolerant once established, making zone 9's long warm season ideal for the extended maturation time this variety requires. Fruit is large, elongated to cylindrical with slight taper, with salmon-pink to apricot-tan skin with a distinctive bluish bloom at full maturity. The flesh is thick, bright orange-yellow, sweet, and very dry — the 'candy roaster' name references the traditional method of roasting the squash in front of a fireplace or in a slow oven until the flesh caramelizes, producing a very sweet, concentrated flavor.

directions: Direct sow in hills 5–6 feet apart; thin to 2 plants per hill. Harvest when skin turns salmon-pink and develops waxy bloom. North Georgia Candy Roaster is one of the most historically significant Appalachian heirloom vegetable varieties — a large winter squash that has been grown in the mountain regions of North Georgia, North Carolina, Tennessee, and adjacent Appalachian areas for centuries, with the variety associated with Cherokee and Scots-Irish Appalachian agricultural traditions of the southern mountain region. The 'candy roaster' name is shared by several closely related Appalachian squash varieties that differ somewhat in size, color, and shape but all share the characteristic of extraordinary sweetness when roasted — the candy-like sweetness that develops when the dense, high-starch flesh is slowly baked or roasted until caramelized. The specific 'North Georgia' designation distinguishes this variety from the similar but distinct 'Tennessee Candy Roaster,' 'Big Orange Candy Roaster,' and other regionally-named selections, with the North Georgia type typically producing cylindrical to elongated fruits with characteristic salmon-pink to apricot skin. The squash can reach enormous sizes: typical fruits are 15–20 lbs, and exceptional specimens exceed 30 lbs, making this one of the largest winter squash varieties in heirloom cultivation. The flesh — thick, dense, bright orange-yellow — is exceptional in nutritional value (very high beta-carotene) and flavor, with a sweetness and complexity that rewards the long growing season required. The variety has been maintained by Appalachian farming families and seed-saving organizations including the Seed Savers Exchange as an important cultural heritage variety of the southeastern mountain region.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — North Georgia Candy Roaster Cherokee heritage squash documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Candy Roaster squash Appalachian heirloom variety notes
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — North Georgia Candy Roaster heritage winter squash description

common_name: Green Striped Cushaw (Heat-Tolerant Southern Cushaw Pumpkin, Gulf Coast Staple Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm (70°F); Green Striped Cushaw is a Cucurbita argyrosperma type — the cushaw species — which has significantly better heat and humidity tolerance than the Cucurbita maxima and C. pepo species, making it one of the most reliable winter-type squash for hot-summer zone 9 and tropical climates. In zone 9, plant April–June for summer production; the heat tolerance allows Cushaw to thrive through Gulf Coast summers that stress or kill other squash species. Fruits develop over 90–110 days and can be harvested young (summer squash stage) or left to mature fully for storage. The large vines (15+ feet) require substantial space. Cushaw's heat tolerance is legendary in Gulf South gardening circles — this is the squash that produces reliably when standard varieties have died from the summer heat.

directions: Direct sow in hills 5–6 feet apart after soil is warm; thin to 2 plants per hill. Harvest when skin pattern develops full color and stem begins to dry. Green Striped Cushaw is the classic variety of Cucurbita argyrosperma — the cushaw species native to Mexico and Central America — that has been grown in the American South for centuries as one of the most heat-tolerant large squash available. The 'cushaw' name is of Native American origin and has been applied to this distinctive squash type in the South since colonial times. The Green Striped Cushaw produces large fruits (10–20 lbs) with a distinctive form: a rounded to oblong base swelling to a thick, curved neck — quite different from the globular or flattened form of most Cucurbita maxima pumpkins. The skin is cream-white with longitudinal green stripes that fade to tan-green at full maturity. The flesh is cream-yellow to pale orange, mild and sweet, with less of the rich pumpkin flavor of darker-fleshed types but with an important practical advantage: it is the squash that can be grown in zone 9's summer heat when virtually all other squash species have died. Cucurbita argyrosperma's heat and humidity tolerance is substantially greater than C. maxima, C. pepo, or C. moschata, and in the historical Gulf South kitchen, cushaw was the standard large winter-storage squash precisely because it produced reliably in the climate where other species failed. Cushaw's mild flesh is used in pies (a Southern tradition), as a cooked vegetable, and in Mexican and Central American cuisines where the species originated.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Green Striped Cushaw Southern heirloom documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Cushaw squash Gulf South variety notes and heat tolerance
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Summer and Winter Squash Variety Selection for the Gulf South
